decks.name: Describing Words
decks.description: Learn how adjectives describe people, places, animals, and things

cards.back: An adjective is a describing word. It tells us more about a noun — like its size, colour, shape, or feeling. For example, in 'a tall building', the word 'tall' is the adjective.

cards.front: What kinds of things can adjectives describe?
cards.back: Adjectives can describe size (big, tiny), colour (red, blue), shape (round, square), feeling (happy, scared), taste (sweet, sour), and many more qualities!
cards.hint: Think about your five senses and feelings

cards.front: Find the adjective: 'The fluffy cat sat on the old mat.'
cards.back: There are two adjectives! 'Fluffy' describes the cat, and 'old' describes the mat. Adjectives come right before the noun they describe.
cards.hint: Which words tell you more about the cat and the mat?

cards.front: Can you use more than one adjective for the same noun?
cards.back: Yes! You can stack adjectives before a noun. For example, 'a big, brown, friendly dog' uses three adjectives to describe the dog.
cards.hint: Think of adding more detail to a picture

cards.front: Where does an adjective usually go in a sentence?
cards.back: An adjective usually comes just before the noun it describes (like 'a brave girl'). It can also come after words like 'is', 'was', or 'are' (like 'The girl is brave').
cards.hint: It can go before the noun or after 'is'

decks.name: Comparison
decks.description: Learn how to compare things using adjectives — bigger, biggest, and more

cards.front: How do we compare two things using adjectives?
cards.back: We add '-er' to short adjectives. For example: tall becomes taller, fast becomes faster. 'Riya is taller than Aman.' This is called the comparative form.
cards.hint: Add two letters to the end of the adjective

cards.front: How do we compare three or more things?
cards.back: We add '-est' to short adjectives. For example: tall becomes tallest, fast becomes fastest. 'Riya is the tallest in the class.' This is called the superlative form.
cards.hint: Add three letters to show the most or the best

cards.front: What do we do with longer adjectives like 'beautiful'?
cards.back: For longer adjectives, we use 'more' for comparing two things and 'most' for three or more. For example: 'more beautiful' and 'most beautiful'. We do NOT say 'beautifuler'!
cards.hint: Use a helper word instead of changing the ending

cards.front: What are the three forms of 'good'?
cards.back: Good, better, best. These are irregular — they change completely instead of just adding -er or -est. 'This cake is good. That cake is better. Mum's cake is the best!'
cards.hint: This one does not follow the normal -er/-est rule

cards.front: What are the three forms of 'bad'?
cards.back: Bad, worse, worst. These are also irregular. 'Monday was bad. Tuesday was worse. Wednesday was the worst day of all!'
cards.hint: Another adjective that breaks the normal rule
